{{short description|Practice of recording information}}
[[Image:Carter-notes-pope-mtg-xl.jpg|thumb|right|U.S. President [[Jimmy Carter|Jimmy Carter's]] notes from his private meeting with [[Pope John Paul II]], October 6, 1979.]]

'''Note-taking''' (sometimes written as '''notetaking''' or '''note taking''') is the practice of recording [[information]] from different sources and platforms. By taking notes, the writer records the essence of the information, freeing their mind from having to [[Recall (memory)|recall]] everything.<ref name="Dror">{{cite journal |last1=Makany |first1=Tamas |last2=Kemp |first2=Jonathan |last3=Dror |first3=Itiel E. |date=2009 |title=Optimising the use of note-taking as an external cognitive aid for increasing learning |journal=[[British Journal of Educational Technology]] |volume=40 |issue=4 |pages=619–635 |doi=10.1111/j.1467-8535.2008.00906.x|url=https://ink.library.smu.edu.sg/lkcsb_research/6652 }}</ref> Notes are commonly drawn from a transient source, such as an oral discussion at a meeting, or a lecture (notes of a meeting are usually called [[minutes]]), in which case the notes may be the only record of the event. Since the advent of [[writing]] and [[literacy]], notes traditionally were almost always [[handwriting|handwritten]] (often in [[notebook]]s), but the introduction of [[comparison of note-taking software|notetaking software]] and [[Website|websites]]<ref>{{Cite web |title=Online Notepad – Write & Save Notes for Free |url=https://onlinenotep.ad/notepad |access-date=2025-05-21 |website=onlinenotep.ad}}</ref> has made digital notetaking possible and widespread. Note-taking is a foundational skill in [[personal knowledge management]].

== History ==

Note-taking has been an important part of human history and scientific development. The Ancient Greeks developed [[hypomnema]], personal records on important subjects. In the [[Renaissance]] and [[early modern period]], students learned to take notes in schools, academies and universities, often producing beautiful volumes that served as reference works after they finished their studies.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Eddy|first1=Matthew Daniel|title=The Nature of Notebooks: How Enlightenment Schoolchildren Transformed the Tabula Rasa|journal=Journal of British Studies|date=2018|volume=57|issue=2|pages=275–307|url=https://www.academia.edu/34448924|doi=10.1017/jbr.2017.239|doi-access=free}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last1=Eddy|first1=Matthew Daniel|title=The Interactive Notebook: How Students Learned to Keep Notes during the Scottish Enlightenment|journal=Book History|date=2016|volume=19|pages=87–131|doi=10.1353/bh.2016.0002|s2cid=151427109|url=https://www.academia.edu/11429336}}</ref> In pre-digital times, people used many kinds of notebooks, including [[commonplace book]]s,<ref>[http://ocp.hul.harvard.edu/reading/commonplace.html Commonplace Books]. Harvard University Library Open Collections Program.</ref> accounting [[waste book]]s, and [[marginalia]].<ref>{{cite book |last1=Gingerich |first1=Owen |title=The Book Nobody Read: Chasing the Revolutions of Nicolaus Copernicus |date=2004-03-01 |publisher=Walker Books |isbn=978-0802714152}}</ref> Philosopher [[John Locke]] developed and published a popular indexing system<ref>{{cite book |last=Locke |first=John |date=1706 |url=http://pds.lib.harvard.edu/pds/view/13925922 |title=A new method of making common-place-books |location=London |publisher=Printed for J. Greenwood, Bookseller, at the end of Cornhil, next Stocks-Market |oclc=3173495}}</ref> which served as a model for commonplace books and inspired at least ten different published editions of commonplace book templates in Europe and the Americas as well as ''Bell's Common-Place Book, Form'd Generally upon the Principles Recommended and Practised by Mr Locke'' (London, 1770).<ref>{{cite book |last1=Havens |first1=Earle |title=Commonplace Books: A History of Manuscripts and Printed Books from Antiquity to the Twentieth Century |date=2001 |publisher=Yale University |pages=55, 58 }}</ref>

== Cognitive psychology ==

Note-taking is a central aspect of a complex human behavior related to information management involving a range of underlying mental processes and their interactions with other cognitive functions.<ref name="piolat">Piolat, A., Olive, T. & Kellogg, R. T. (2005). Cognitive effort during note-taking. Applied Cognitive Psychology, 19, 291–312.</ref> The person taking notes must acquire and filter the incoming sources, organize and restructure existing knowledge structures, comprehend and write down their explanation of the information, and ultimately store and integrate the freshly processed material. The result is a knowledge representation, and a [[Storage (memory)|memory storage]].<ref name="Dror"/> Studies comparing the performance of students who took handwritten notes to students who typed their notes found that students who took handwritten notes performed better on examinations, hypothetically due to the deeper processing of learned material through selective rephrasing instead of word-for-word transcription which is common when typing notes.<ref>{{citation|title=Notes on Note-Taking: Review of Research and Insights for Students and Instructors|author=Michael C. Friedman|publisher=Harvard Initiative for Learning and Teaching, Harvard University|url=https://hilt.harvard.edu/files/hilt/files/notetaking_0.pdf|date=October 15, 2014|access-date=January 31, 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180218171829/http://hilt.harvard.edu/files/hilt/files/notetaking_0.pdf|archive-date=February 18, 2018|url-status=dead}}</ref>

== Reasons for note-taking ==

Note-taking is a good strategy to enhance learning and memory,<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Jansen |first1=Renée S. |last2=Lakens |first2=Daniel |last3=IJsselsteijn |first3=Wijnand A. |date=2017-11-01 |title=An integrative review of the cognitive costs and benefits of note-taking |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1747938X17300374 |journal=Educational Research Review |volume=22 |pages=223–233 |doi=10.1016/j.edurev.2017.10.001 |issn=1747-938X}}</ref> as it allows the notetaker to be selective and reorganize ideas during a lecture.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last1=Van Meter |first1=Peggy |last2=Yokoi |first2=Linda |last3=Pressley |first3=Michael |date=September 1994 |title=College students' theory of note-taking derived from their perceptions of note-taking. |url=http://doi.apa.org/getdoi.cfm?doi=10.1037/0022-0663.86.3.323 |journal=Journal of Educational Psychology |language=en |volume=86 |issue=3 |pages=323–338 |doi=10.1037/0022-0663.86.3.323 |issn=1939-2176|url-access=subscription }}</ref> These notes can be reworded in an easier way that will facilitate the understanding of the content taught in class. Afterward, the notes can be used to help process, recall, and use information that has been rapidly gone through during the lecture.<ref name=":0" />

Taking notes on different words used in lectures fosters vocabulary learning and stimulates autonomous learning of actively engaged individuals.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Jin |first1=Zhouhan |last2=Webb |first2=Stuart |date=2021-12-14 |title=Does writing words in notes contribute to vocabulary learning? |url=http://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/13621688211062184 |journal=Language Teaching Research |volume=29 |issue=2 |language=en |pages=560–587 |doi=10.1177/13621688211062184 |issn=1362-1688|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

== Systems ==

Many different formats are used to structure information and make it easier to find and to understand later. The format of the initial record may often be informal and/or unstructured. One common format for such notes is [[shorthand]], which can allow large amounts of information to be put on [[paper]] very quickly. Historically, note-taking was an analog process, written in [[notebook]]s, or other paper methods like [[Post-It]] notes. In the digital age, use of [[computer]]s, [[Tablet computer|tablet PC]]s and [[personal digital assistant]]s (PDAs) is common.

The note taker usually has to work fast, and different note-taking styles and techniques try to make the best use of time. The average rate of speech is 2–3 words per second (which is 120-180 words per minute), but the average handwriting speed as only 0.2–0.3 words per second (which is 12-18 words per minute).<ref name="piolat"/>

Regardless of the medium, note-taking can be broadly divided into linear and nonlinear methods, which can be combined.

Regardless of the system used, it can be best to focus on writing down the most important information first.

== Linear note-taking ==

Linear note-taking is the process of recording information in the order in which you receive it. '''Linear notes'''<!--boldface per [[WP:R#PLA]]--> are typically chronological outlines of a lecture or a text. Linear note taking is a common means of taking notes, however, the potential to just transcribe everything that is being said or on the presentation slide is quite high.

===Outlining===
Outlining is a common note-taking system.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Laskey |first1=Marcia L. |last2=Gibson |first2=Paula W. |date=1997 |chapter=Outlining |title=College Study Strategies: Thinking and Learning |location=Boston |publisher=[[Allyn and Bacon]] |pages=[https://archive.org/details/collegestudystra0000lask/page/141 141–145] |isbn=0205191525 |oclc=35184071 |chapter-url=https://archive.org/details/collegestudystra0000lask/page/141 |chapter-url-access=registration}}</ref> Notes and thoughts are organized in a structured, logical manner, reducing the time needed to edit and review, allowing a lot of information to be digested in a short period of time. For classes that involve many formulas and graphs, like mathematics or [[chemistry]], a system such as [[Cornell Notes]] may be better.

[[Outline (list)|Outlines]] generally proceed down a page, using headings and [[Bullet (typography)|bullets]] to structure information. A common system consists of [[Subject heading|heading]]s that use [[Roman numerals]], letters of the [[alphabet]], and [[Arabic numerals]] at different levels.  A typical structure would be:

:I. First main topic
::A. Subtopic
::# point 1
::# point 2
::#point 3
::B. Subtopic 
::# point 1
::#point 2
::#point 3
:II. Second main topic
::A. Subtopic
::# point 1
::#point 2
::#point 3
::B. Subtopic
::# point 1
::#point 2
::#point 3

However, this sort of structure has limitations in non-digital form since it is difficult to go back and insert more information. Adaptive systems are used for paper-and-pen insertions, such as using the reverse side of the preceding page in a spiral notebook to make insertions. Or one can simply leave large spaces in between items, to enable more material to be inserted. (For information about application software that supports outlining, see [[:Category:Outliners]].)

Computerized note-taking, whether with a [[word processor]], outliner software, or a digital notebook program, allows note-takers to revise easily and add more entries or rows to the outline.

===Sentence method===
Sentence note-taking is simply writing down each topic as a short, simple sentence. This method works well for fast-paced lesson where a lot of information is being covered. The note-taker records every new thought, fact, or topic on a separate line. All information is recorded but is not organized into major and minor topics. Notes can be numbered or set off with bullets showing where a new thought begins.

== Non-linear note-taking ==

Approaches to non-linear note-taking include clustering,<ref>Rico, G. L. (1983). Writing the natural way: using right-brain techniques to release your expressive powers. New York: Penguin Putnam.</ref> [[concept mapping]],<ref>Canas, A. J., Coffey, J. W., Carnot, M. J., Feltovich, P., Hoffman, R. R., Feltovich, J. et al. (2003). A summary of literature pertaining to the use of concept mapping techniques and technologies for education and performance support. Report to the Chief of Naval Education and Training Pensacola FL 32500.</ref><ref>Novak, J. D. & Gowin, D. B. (1984). Learning how to learn. New York: Cambridge University Press.</ref> [[Cornell Notes]],<ref>Pauk, W. (2001). How to study in college. Boston, MA: Houghton Mifflin.</ref> idea mapping,<ref>Nast, J. (2006). Idea mapping: how to access your hidden brain power, learn faster, remember more, and achieve success in business. Hoboken, NJ: John Wiley & Sons.</ref> instant replays,<ref>Turley, J. (1989). Speed-reading in business. An action plan for success. Menlo Park, CA: Crisp Publications.</ref> [[Ishikawa diagrams]],<ref>Ishikawa, K. (1984). Guide to quality control (2nd revised English ed.). New York: Unipub.</ref> [[Cognitive map|knowledge maps]],<ref>O'Donnell, A. M., Dansereau, D. F. & Hall, R. F. (2002). Knowledge maps as scaffolds for cognitive processing. ''[[Educational Psychology Review]]'', 14, 71–86.</ref> learning maps,<ref>Rose, C. & Nicholl, M. J. (1997). Accelerated learning for the 21st century: the six-step plan to unlock your master mind. London: Judy Piatkus Publishers.</ref>  [[mind map]]ping,<ref>Buzan, T. (2000). Use your head. Harlow, England: BBC Active.; Catchpole, R. & Garland, N. (1996). Mind maps: using research to improve the student learning experience. In G. Gibbs (Ed.), Improving student learning: using research to improve student learning (pp. 211–222). Oxford: Oxford Centre for Staff Development at Oxford Brookes University.; Gruneberg, M. M. & Mathieson, M. (1997). The perceived value of minds maps (spider diagrams) as learning and memory aids. Cognitive Technology, 2, 21–24.; Hartley, J. (2002). Note-taking in non-academic settings: a review. Applied Cognitive Psychology, 16, 559–574.</ref> model maps,<ref>Caviglioli, O. & Harris, I. (2000). Mapwise accelerated learning through visible thinking. Strafford, NH: Network Educational Press.</ref> and the pyramid principle.<ref>Minto, B. (1987). The pyramid principle. Harlow, England: Financial Times, Prentice Hall, Pearson Education.</ref>

===Charting===
The charting method of note taking,<ref>{{cite book |last1=Laskey |first1=Marcia L. |last2=Gibson |first2=Paula W. |date=1997 |chapter=Charting |title=College Study Strategies: Thinking and Learning |location=Boston |publisher=[[Allyn and Bacon]] |pages=[https://archive.org/details/collegestudystra0000lask/page/145 145–153] |isbn=0205191525 |oclc=35184071 |chapter-url=https://archive.org/details/collegestudystra0000lask/page/145 |chapter-url-access=registration}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |last1=Nist-Olejnik |first1=Sherrie |last2=Holschuh |first2=Jodi |date=2014 |chapter=Charting |title=College Success Strategies |edition=4th |location=New York |publisher=[[Pearson Education]] |page=[https://archive.org/details/collegesuccessst0000nist/page/140 140] |isbn=9780205190911 |oclc=740250682 |chapter-url=https://archive.org/details/collegesuccessst0000nist/page/140 |chapter-url-access=registration}}</ref> which includes the drawing of [[Table (information)|table]]s sometimes called {{em|study frames}},<ref>{{cite book |last=Pacheco |first=Beth |date=1985 |chapter=Charts and Study Frames |title=Academic Reading and Study Skills: A Theme-Centered Approach |edition=1st |location=New York |publisher=[[Holt, Rinehart and Winston]] |pages=[https://archive.org/details/academicreadings0000pach/page/87 87–98] |isbn=0030620341 |oclc=10429683 |chapter-url=https://archive.org/details/academicreadings0000pach/page/87 |chapter-url-access=registration}}</ref> is useful for subject matter that can be broken into categories, such as similarities, differences, date, event, impact, etc. Students may use charting to identify categories and draw a table prior to a lecture or may review and rewrite notes using the charting method.

===Mapping===
[[Image:Mindmap.gif|thumb|right|A mind map connects ideas together graphically]]
Mapping uses spatial organization and diagrams to assemble information.<ref name=mapping>{{cite book |last1=Laskey |first1=Marcia L. |last2=Gibson |first2=Paula W. |date=1997 |chapter=Mapping |title=College Study Strategies: Thinking and Learning |location=Boston |publisher=[[Allyn and Bacon]] |pages=[https://archive.org/details/collegestudystra0000lask/page/135 135–141] |isbn=0205191525 |oclc=35184071 |chapter-url=https://archive.org/details/collegestudystra0000lask/page/135 |chapter-url-access=registration}}</ref> Ideas are written in a node–link structure, with lines connecting ideas together.<ref name=mapping/> [[Mind map]]s are drawn in a [[tree structure]] from a central point, purpose, or goal in the center of the page and then branch outward to identify all the ideas connected to that goal.<ref name=mindmapping/> Colors, small graphics, and symbols are often used to help to visualize the information more easily. It is also used for planning and writing essays.<ref name=mindmapping>{{Cite journal|last=Cristine|first=Goldberg|date=November 2004|title=Brain Friendly Techniques: Mind Mapping.|url=https://eric.ed.gov/?id=EJ720682|journal=School Library Media Activities Monthly|language=en|volume=21|issue=3|issn=0889-9371}}</ref>

===Cornell Notes===
{{Main|Cornell Notes}}
[[File:NotesCornell.png|thumb|A diagram showing the three sections of a page of Cornell notes]]
The Cornell Notes method of note-taking was developed by [[Walter Pauk]] of [[Cornell University]] and promoted in his bestselling 1974 book ''How to Study in College''. It is commonly used at universities today. The Cornell method consists of dividing a single page into three sections: a right-hand column for notes, a left-hand column for cues, and a strip at the bottom for a summary. Cues are key words or questions that help evoke key aspects of the topic. Cornell notes may be more effective for understanding concepts or producing readable notes, but studies have found that they had no significant effect on student performance.<ref>Jacobs, Keil. ''[http://soar.wichita.edu/dspace/bitstream/handle/10057/1388/grasp-2008-56.pdf?sequence=1 A Comparison of Two Note Taking Methods in a Secondary English Classroom]'' Proceedings: 4th Annual Symposium: Graduate Research and Scholarly Projects [79]
Conference proceedings held at the Eugene Hughes Metropolitan Complex, Wichita State University, April 25, 2008. Symposium Chair: David M. Eichhorn</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://yourspace.minotstateu.edu/laurie.geller/Capstone%20Examples/Final%20Capstone%20Projects/Duane%20Broe%20Capstone%20Project.pdf|title=The Effects of Teaching Cornell Notes on Student Achievement|last=Broe|first=Duane|date=Summer 2013|website=www.minotstateu.edu/#|access-date=2017-05-12|archive-date=2020-07-09|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200709230017/http://yourspace.minotstateu.edu/laurie.geller/Capstone%20Examples/Final%20Capstone%20Projects/Duane%20Broe%20Capstone%20Project.pdf|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |last1=Quintus |first1=Lori |last2=Borr |first2=Mari |last3=Duffield |first3=Stacy |last4=Napoleon |first4=Larry |last5=Welch |first5=Anita |title=The Impact of the Cornell Note-Taking Method on Students' Performance in a High School Family and Consumer Sciences Class |url=http://www.natefacs.org/Pages/v30no1/v30no1Quintus.pdf |website=www.natefacs.org |date=Spring–Summer 2012|access-date=2018-10-10}}</ref><ref>{{Cite thesis|last=Zulejka|first=Baharev|title=The effects of Cornell note-taking and review strategies on recall and comprehension of lecture content for middle school students with and without disabilities|date=2016|publisher=Rutgers University - Graduate School of Education|url=https://rucore.libraries.rutgers.edu/rutgers-lib/51151/|doi=10.7282/T3HD7XZ8|language=en}}</ref>

===SQ3R===
[[SQ3R]] ("Survey, Question, Read, Recite, Review") is a method of taking notes from written material, though it might be better classified as a method of reading and gaining understanding. The reader skims the written material to produce a list of headings (Survey), which are then converted into questions (Question). The reader then considers the questions while reading to provide motivation for what is being covered (Read). The reader writes notes in sections headed by the questions (Recite), then writes a summary from memory and reviews the notes (Review).

Research shows that students who use the SQ3R strategy retain more information and achieve higher test scores.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Carlston|first=David L.|date=2011-06-14|title=Benefits of Student-Generated Note Packets|journal=Teaching of Psychology|language=en|volume=38|issue=3|pages=142–146|doi=10.1177/0098628311411786|s2cid=145306500|issn=0098-6283}}</ref>

An updated version called [[SQ3R|SQ4R]],<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://brazosport.edu/programs/academics/Learning-Frameworks/SQ4R/index|title=SQ4R Reading Method|website=brazosport.edu|language=en|access-date=2018-03-26}}</ref> which adds a "Relate" step before "Review", has been used by some students since the early 1960s.

=== Guided notes ===
Sometimes lecturers may provide handouts of guided notes, which provide a "map" of the lecture content with key points or ideas missing. Students then fill in missing items as the lecture progresses. Guided notes may assist students in following lectures and identifying the most important ideas from a lecture. This format provides students with a framework, yet requires active listening (as opposed to providing copies of presentation slides in their entirety), and promotes active engagement during lecture or [[independent reading]]. The student ends up with full and accurate notes for use as a study guide.

Research suggests that guided notes improve student recording of critical points in lecture, as well as quiz scores on related content. In addition, an investigation carried out on students with learning problems showed that the use of the guided notes is an effective strategy to improve the performance of these students.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Hamilton|first1=Sheri L.|last2=Seibert|first2=Marilyn A.|last3=Gardner|first3=Ralph|last4=Talbert-Johnson|first4=Carolyn|date=May 2000|title=Using Guided Notes to Improve the Academic Achievement of Incarcerated Adolescents with Learning and Behavior Problems|journal=Remedial and Special Education|volume=21|issue=3|pages=133–170|doi=10.1177/074193250002100302|s2cid=143857611|issn=0741-9325}}</ref>

=== Card file ===
{{Main|Card file}}
A [[card file]] uses individual notes on [[index card]]s (or their digital equivalent) that may be linked to each other through [[Index term|subject headings]] or other [[metadata]] such as numbers and [[Tag (metadata)|tags]].<ref>{{cite book |last=Eco |first=Umberto |author-link=Umberto Eco |date=2015 |orig-year=1977 |chapter=The Work Plan and the Index Cards |title=How to Write a Thesis |location=Cambridge, MA |publisher=[[MIT Press]] |pages=107–144 |isbn=9780262527132 |oclc=897401730 |jstor=j.ctt17kk9g5.10 |doi=10.7551/mitpress/10029.003.0008}}</ref>

==Electronic note-taking methods==
{{see also|Electronic notetaking}}
The growing use of laptops in universities and colleges has led to a rise in electronic note-taking. Many students write their notes in [[word processor]]s or prepare digital hand-written notes using a [[graphics tablet]] or [[tablet computer]] and [[stylus|styli]] or [[digital pen]]s, with the aid of note-taking [[software]]. Online applications are receiving growing attention from students who can forward notes using email, or otherwise make use of collaborative features in these applications and can also download the texts as a file on a local computer. It has also become common for [[lecturer]]s to deliver [[lecture]]s using these and similar technologies, including [[electronic whiteboard]]s, especially at [[Institute of technology|institutes of technology]].{{citation needed|date=September 2019}}

Online note-taking has created problems for teachers who must balance educational [[Freedom of speech in the United States|freedom]] with copyright and intellectual property concerns regarding course content.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.mla.org/About-Us/Governance/Committees/Committee-Listings/Professional-Issues/Committee-on-Academic-Freedom-and-Professional-Rights-and-Responsibilities/Academic-Freedom-and-Faculty-Intellectual-Property-in-the-Era-of-Zoom-and-Learning-Management-Systems|access-date=2024-06-08|language=en|title=Academic Freedom and Faculty Intellectual Property in the Era of Zoom and Learning Management Systems|website=www.mla.org|publisher=[[Modern Language Association]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.aaup.org/sites/default/files/Remote%20Teaching%20and%20IP%20Concerns%20Webinar%20December%202020.pdf|access-date=2024-06-08|language=en|title=Remote Teaching, Recording of Classes and Intellectual Property|type=PowerPoint presentation|website=www.aaup.org|last1=Nisenson|first1=Aaron|last2=Sinclair|first2=Chris}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=https://library.torontomu.ca/copyright/faculty/copyright-faqs/my-teaching-materials-have-been-posted-online/|access-date=2024-06-08|language=en |title=My Teaching Materials have been posted online FAQ |date=6 February 2018 |website=library.torontomu.ca |publisher=[[Toronto Metropolitan University Library]]}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.insidehighered.com/news/2020/05/19/who-owns-all-course-content-youre-putting-online|access-date=2024-06-08|language=en |title=IP Problems |work=[[Inside Higher Ed]] |last=Flaherty |first=Colleen}}</ref>

Electronic note-taking may be less effective than traditional methods of note-taking. A study done by Pam A. Mueller of Princeton University and Daniel M. Oppenheimer of the University of California, Los Angeles showed that students who take notes digitally retain less information than students who take notes on paper, and the digital note-takers have more difficulty remembering what they've written.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Mueller |first1=Pam A. |last2=Oppenheimer |first2=Daniel M. |date=2014 |title=The Pen Is Mightier Than the Keyboard: Advantages of Longhand Over Laptop Note Taking |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/24543504 |journal=Psychological Science |volume=25 |issue=6 |pages=1159–1168 |doi=10.1177/0956797614524581 |jstor=24543504 |pmid=24760141 |issn=0956-7976}}</ref> However, there was also a study performed by Linlin Luo, Kenneth Kiewra, Abraham Flanigan, and Markeya Peteranetz presented evidence to support that electronic note-taking did show benefits in certain stages of learning as well.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Luo |first1=Linlin |last2=Kiewra |first2=Kenneth A. |last3=Flanigan |first3=Abraham E. |last4=Peteranetz |first4=Markeya S. |date=2018 |title=Laptop versus longhand note taking: effects on lecture notes and achievement |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/45213865 |journal=Instructional Science |volume=46 |issue=6 |pages=947–971 |doi=10.1007/s11251-018-9458-0 |jstor=45213865 |issn=0020-4277}}</ref> Electronic note-taking has created computer-aided distractions in class as multitasking on laptops is very easy to accomplish.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Cain|first1=J|last2=Bird|first2=ER|last3=Jones|first3=MK|date=2008|title=Mobile computing initiatives within pharmacy education.|pmc=4812780|journal=Am J Pharm Educ|volume=4|issue=7|pages=72|pmid=27168620|doi=10.5688/ajpe797107}}</ref> However, this research only applies to typing notes on laptops, not writing on tablets.

== Professional services ==

Professional note-takers provide access to information for people who cannot take their own notes, such as some [[deaf]] and [[hearing impaired]] people. They most frequently work in [[colleges]] and [[universities]], but are also used in workplace meetings, appointments, conferences, and training sessions.{{citation needed|date=March 2019}}
